metadata.title: Nooto — thought to action.
metadata.description: Nooto summarizes your meetings & conversations, creates tasks, reminders & memories. Works on phone, desktop and all wearables.
metadata.productDescription: Nooto is a wearable AI that captures conversations, creates summaries, tasks, and memories.
metadata.privacyDescription: At Nooto, your privacy and the security of your data are our top priorities.
metadata.docsDescription: Nooto developer documentation, guides, and API reference.

hero.heading: Personal intelligence that turns thought to action.
hero.description: Nooto captures your meetings and conversations, creates summaries, tasks, and memories — across every device you own.

manifesto.introHeading: We are building personal intelligence that thinks
manifesto.statement1Heading: The boundary between thought and machine
manifesto.statement1Body: We are at the beginning of a new era — intelligent systems that listen, reason, and act.
manifesto.statement2Body: Ideas shared. Decisions made. Promises kept. Yet most of it fades before you can act on it.
manifesto.statement3Body: Nooto captures the nuance of every conversation and turns scattered words into structured action.
manifesto.statement4Body: A wearable device paired with deeply personal AI — built to extend your memory and amplify your intent.
manifesto.statement5Body: Designed not to replace your thinking, but to free your mind for what matters most.

productShowcase.heading: Your meetings,
productShowcase.description: Nooto automatically captures your conversations and transforms them into structured summaries, action items, and searchable transcripts.

mockup.title: Team Discusses Inbound Sales and Demo Process
mockup.rewindDescription: Search across all your past conversations

howItWorks.heading: How it works
howItWorks.description: From conversation to action in three simple steps.
howItWorks.step1Description: Wear Nooto during meetings, conversations, or brainstorming sessions. It listens and captures everything in the background.
howItWorks.step2Description: AI transcribes, summarizes, and extracts key insights, action items, and important moments from your conversations.
howItWorks.step3Description: Get organized summaries, automatic task creation, smart reminders, and searchable memories — all synced across your devices.

meetNooto.heading: Meet
meetNooto.pendantDescription: Lightweight pendant that captures conversations and turns them into actionable insights.
meetNooto.glassDescription: See the world differently. AI-powered smart glasses with real-time transcription.
meetNooto.chooseDevice: Beautiful hardware, powerful AI. Choose the device that fits your life.

features.worksWithEveryDeviceDescription: Nooto App works seamlessly with your existing device — no need to buy anything new!
features.thousandsOfAppsDescription: Apps for Productivity, Relationships, Health, Companionship and more — all on one device.

cta.heading: Ready to never forget
cta.description: Join thousands of professionals who use Nooto to stay on top of every meeting and conversation.

product.heroDescription: A wearable AI companion that listens, understands, and acts — so you never miss what matters.
product.askDescription: Share your ideas or ask anything that's on your mind.
product.learnDescription: Nooto will answer, knowing everything about you.
product.doDescription: Translate, send emails, summarize, plan and more using voice.
product.captureFeature5: On & offline: live stream or offline recording
product.recallFeature2: Ask Nooto AI: knows you + web search
product.recallFeature6: API & MCP: work with your data
product.automateFeature1: Sync tasks to your favorite task manager
product.automateFeature4: Nooto App Store: thousands of tools
product.appsDescription: Turn conversations into actions across your tools.
product.testimonial1Text: Completely changed how I handle meetings. I never miss an action item anymore.
product.testimonial2Text: The AI summaries are shockingly accurate. It's like having a perfect memory.
product.testimonial3Text: Best productivity purchase I've made. My team adopted it within a week.
product.faq1A: Nooto transcribes conversations, generates summaries with action items, supports 25+ languages, works online and offline, and connects to thousands of apps through its marketplace.
product.faq2A: The device is a one-time purchase. It includes an unlimited free plan with on-device transcription, plus free cloud minutes every month. Unlimited cloud transcription is available with a subscription.
product.faq3A: The Nooto device, protective silicone case, neck lanyard, magnetic charging cable (USB-C), and a quick start guide.
product.faq4A: Your data is encrypted in transit (TLS) and at rest (AES-256). Nooto can run locally so your data never leaves your device. You can export or delete everything at any time.
product.faq5A: Yes, Nooto works with all iPhone and Android models. There's also a Mac desktop app and a web app for any browser.
product.faq6A: No. Nooto includes a generous free plan. You can upgrade anytime for unlimited cloud transcription.
product.bottomCtaDescription: Join thousands of professionals who never forget a conversation.
product.transcriptLine1: Speaker 1: We need to revisit the Q4 targets...
product.transcriptLine2: Speaker 2: Agreed, the pipeline looks strong but...
product.transcriptLine3: Speaker 1: Let me share the updated forecast...
product.searchPlaceholder: What did we decide about the Q4 budget?

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: At Nooto, your privacy and the security of your data are our top priorities. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your information.
privacy.yourDataDesc: We only collect what's necessary to provide and improve our services.
privacy.yourControlDesc: You decide how your data is used and can manage your preferences at any time.
privacy.yourSecurityDesc: Advanced encryption and security protocols protect your information.
privacy.personalInfoDesc: Includes your name, email, and contact details, necessary for account creation and management.
privacy.conversationalDataDesc: We record and store your conversations to offer tailored AI feedback and enrich your personal memory bank. Audio data is processed to generate transcripts, summaries, and action items.
privacy.usageDataDesc: Insights into how you interact with our app, like feature usage and session duration, are used for continuous improvement of our services.
privacy.infoUseIntro: We are dedicated to using your information responsibly:
privacy.serviceEnhancementDesc: To offer state-of-the-art conversation recording, AI-driven analysis, and efficient memory bank services.
privacy.userCommunicationDesc: For updates, support, and promotional content (with your consent).
privacy.innovationResearchDesc: Continuously researching and analyzing data to improve functionality.
privacy.robustStorageDesc: Your data is stored on highly secure servers, safeguarded with advanced technology and regular security audits.
privacy.uncomprSecurityDesc: We employ the latest security protocols including end-to-end encryption to prevent data breaches and protect against unauthorized access.
privacy.selectiveSharingDesc: We collaborate with third-party providers under stringent confidentiality agreements, ensuring they adhere to our privacy standards. These providers assist with hosting, analytics, and AI processing.
privacy.legalComplianceDesc: We may disclose your information if required by law, always respecting your privacy rights and notifying you when legally permitted.
privacy.accessUpdateDesc: View and modify your personal information at any time through your account settings.
privacy.chooseDataUseDesc: Opt out of non-essential data uses, like marketing communications and analytics.
privacy.dataDeletionDesc: Request the complete removal of your data, within legal and operational requirements.
privacy.wearableDesc: We offer an alternative version of our app that integrates with wearable devices. In this version, no data is collected by Nooto. You bring your own API keys, and all data is stored locally on your device. This ensures complete control over your data and privacy.
privacy.policyUpdatesDesc: We reserve the right to modify this policy. Changes will be communicated transparently on this page, along with the revision date. We encourage you to review this policy periodically.
privacy.contactCardDesc: For any inquiries regarding our data practices or this policy, please contact us at {email}

docs.comingSoonDesc: This documentation page is being written. Check back soon or contribute on
docs.introDescription: Create apps that extend Nooto's capabilities — from custom AI personalities to real-time integrations.
docs.introContent:
## What Are Nooto Apps?

Nooto apps are modular extensions that augment the core functionality of the Nooto platform. They can modify AI behavior, analyze conversations, and connect with external services. Apps are installed by users from the Nooto App Store at https://apps.nooto.app and run alongside the core Nooto experience.

- **Customize AI Behavior** — Create custom AI personalities, alter conversation styles, or add specialized knowledge domains
- **Process Memories** — Customize how conversations are analyzed, summarized, and categorized after they end
- **Real-time Processing** — Process live transcripts as conversations happen and react in real time
- **External Integrations** — Connect Nooto to Slack, Notion, GitHub, CRMs, and any external service via webhooks
- **Chat Tools** — Add custom tools the AI can invoke on behalf of users during conversations
- **Audio Streaming** — Receive raw audio bytes for custom speech-to-text, voice activity detection, or audio analysis
- **Earn Revenue** — Publish to the app store and monetize your creations

## App Types

Nooto apps fall into two categories based on whether they need a server.

### Prompt-Based Apps (No Server Required)

These apps consist entirely of a text prompt. No backend, no hosting, no code. There are two subtypes:

- **Chat Prompts** — Injected into Nooto's system prompt to alter its conversational style. Create expert personas, custom assistants, or specialized advisors. Example: a fitness coach that estimates calories from meal descriptions.
- **Memory Prompts** — Applied after a conversation ends to customize how the memory is analyzed. Extract specific fields, categorize by topic, or reformat summaries. Example: extract action items with owners and deadlines from every meeting.

### Integration Apps (Webhook-Based)

These apps require a server endpoint that receives HTTP POST requests from Nooto. There are four subtypes:

- **Memory Creation Triggers** — Your endpoint is called whenever a new memory is created. Use this to sync summaries to Slack, update a CRM, or push tasks to a project manager.
- **Real-time Transcript Processors** — Your endpoint receives live transcript segments as a conversation happens. Use this for live coaching, keyword alerts, or real-time translation.
- **Chat Tools** — You define tool schemas that Nooto's AI can call during conversations. The AI sends structured requests to your endpoint and incorporates the result in its response.
- **Audio Streaming** — Your endpoint receives raw PCM16 audio bytes over a WebSocket connection. Use this for custom ASR engines, voice biometrics, or audio analysis.

## Capabilities at a Glance

- **Chat Prompts** — Alter conversational style, create personas (no server)
- **Memory Prompts** — Customize memory analysis and summaries (no server)
- **Memory Triggers** — React to new memories with webhooks (server required)
- **Real-time Transcripts** — Process live speech segments (server required)
- **Chat Tools** — Add callable tools to the AI (server required)
- **Audio Streaming** — Receive raw audio over WebSocket (server required)

## Quick Start: Build Your First App in 5 Minutes

You can test the full integration flow without writing any server code using webhook.site.

1. Go to webhook.site and copy your unique URL
2. Download the Nooto app from the App Store or Google Play
3. In the Nooto app, go to Explore and tap Create an App
4. Select a capability (for example, Real-time Transcript), paste your webhook.site URL as the endpoint, and save
5. Install the app on your account and start speaking
6. Watch real-time data appear on webhook.site as Nooto sends transcript segments to your URL

This lets you inspect the exact JSON payloads Nooto sends before you write a single line of code.

## Developer Mode

Enable Developer Mode in the Nooto app (Settings > Developer Mode) to access development features:

- **Test your apps** without submitting them to the store
- **View request logs** to debug webhook payloads and responses
- **Sideload apps** by URL during development
- **Toggle apps on/off** quickly for A/B testing

## Example Apps

Learn from real apps built by the community:

- **Hey Nooto** — Ask questions and get answers via notification in real-time
- **Nooto Mentor** — Proactive AI mentor that provides guidance during conversations
- **Slack Integration** — Post conversation summaries and memories to Slack channels
- **Zapier** — Connect Nooto to 5000+ apps through Zapier automations
- **GitHub** — Create issues and manage PRs from your conversations

Browse the full catalog at https://apps.nooto.app
docs.promptBasedDescription: Create apps that customize AI personality and memory processing — no server required.
docs.promptBasedContent:
## Overview

Prompt-based apps let you customize Nooto's AI behavior without any server infrastructure. You write a text prompt, and Nooto injects it into the appropriate stage of its pipeline. There are two types:

- **Chat Prompts** — Change how Nooto responds in conversations
- **Memory Prompts** — Customize how memories are analyzed and summarized

## Chat Prompts

Chat prompts alter Nooto's conversational personality and knowledge base. When a user enables your app, your prompt is appended to Nooto's system prompt for every conversation.

### How It Works

Nooto's AI uses a system prompt that defines its base behavior. Your chat prompt is appended to this system prompt, allowing you to:

- Define a specific persona or role for the AI
- Add domain-specific knowledge or terminology
- Set conversation rules and boundaries
- Control response format, length, and tone

The user's existing conversation context and memories are still available to the AI. Your prompt adds to this, it does not replace it.

### Creating a Chat Prompt App

1. Open the Nooto app
2. Go to Explore and tap Create an App
3. Select Chat Prompt as the capability
4. Write your system prompt in the text field
5. Test by enabling the app and chatting with Nooto
6. When satisfied, submit for review

### Example: Fitness Coach

You are a certified fitness coach and nutritionist. When the user discusses meals, provide calorie estimates and nutritional advice. When they mention workouts, suggest improvements and track their progress over time using their conversation history. Always be encouraging but honest about areas for improvement. Format your responses with clear sections: Assessment, Recommendations, and Next Steps.

### Example: Meeting Facilitator

You are a meeting facilitator. When reviewing conversation transcripts, identify: who spoke the most and who was quiet, whether the meeting had a clear agenda, if decisions were reached or deferred, and any action items that were implied but not explicitly stated. Provide a facilitation score from 1-10 with specific improvement suggestions.

### Example: Technical Interviewer

You are a senior technical interviewer. When the user describes a coding problem or system design question, evaluate their approach for correctness, scalability, and code quality. Point out edge cases they missed. Suggest follow-up questions that would test deeper understanding. Rate their answer on a scale and explain what a strong candidate would add.

### Best Practices for Chat Prompts

- **Be specific about the role** — "You are a certified financial advisor specializing in retirement planning" works better than "You are a finance expert"
- **Include output format** — Tell the AI exactly how to structure responses (bullet points, sections, scoring rubrics)
- **Provide examples** — Show the AI one or two ideal responses so it understands your expectations
- **Set boundaries** — Explicitly state what the AI should decline to do (e.g., "Do not provide specific stock recommendations")
- **Keep it focused** — A single, clear persona outperforms a prompt that tries to cover multiple roles
- **Test with diverse inputs** — Try your prompt with different conversation types to ensure it behaves consistently

## Memory Prompts

Memory prompts customize how Nooto processes and categorizes your conversation memories. After a conversation ends, Nooto creates a structured memory. Your prompt controls what gets extracted and how.

### How It Works

When a conversation ends, Nooto runs its memory extraction pipeline. Your memory prompt is injected into this pipeline, allowing you to:

- Define custom categories for different conversation types
- Specify exactly which fields to extract (action items, decisions, questions, commitments)
- Control the format and structure of the summary
- Add conditional logic (e.g., "If this is a sales call, extract the deal size and next steps")

The full transcript is available to the AI when your memory prompt runs.

### Creating a Memory Prompt App

1. Open the Nooto app
2. Go to Explore and tap Create an App
3. Select Memory Prompt as the capability
4. Write your extraction prompt
5. Have a test conversation and check the resulting memory
6. Iterate on your prompt until the extraction meets your needs

### Example: Meeting Notes Extractor

For every conversation, extract the following in structured format:
- Meeting participants (names and roles if mentioned)
- Agenda items discussed
- Key decisions made (with who made them)
- Action items with owners and deadlines
- Open questions or unresolved topics
- Follow-up date if mentioned
- Overall meeting effectiveness: was the goal achieved?

### Example: Sales Call Analyzer

Analyze this sales conversation and extract:
- Prospect name and company
- Pain points mentioned
- Budget signals (explicit or implied)
- Timeline expectations
- Competitors mentioned
- Objections raised and how they were handled
- Buying signals (positive and negative)
- Recommended next steps
- Deal stage: Discovery, Qualification, Proposal, Negotiation, or Closed

### Example: Learning Journal

From this conversation, extract everything I can learn:
- New concepts or ideas introduced
- Key facts or statistics mentioned
- Book, article, or resource recommendations
- Counterarguments to my existing views
- Questions I should research further
- A one-paragraph summary of the most important insight

### Best Practices for Memory Prompts

- **Be explicit about fields** — List every field you want extracted, do not rely on the AI to infer
- **Specify format** — Tell the AI whether you want bullet points, paragraphs, or structured sections
- **Handle edge cases** — Include instructions for when fields are not present (e.g., "If no action items, write None")
- **Test with real conversations** — Synthetic test data often misses the messiness of real speech
- **Keep it under 500 words** — Shorter, focused prompts produce more consistent results than long, complex ones

## Testing with Developer Mode

Enable Developer Mode in Settings to test your prompt-based apps:

1. Create your app and install it on your own account
2. Start a conversation (or use a previous one for memory prompts)
3. Check the results: for chat prompts, talk to Nooto; for memory prompts, review the generated memory
4. Edit your prompt and re-test until the results are satisfactory
5. When ready, submit for review from your app's settings page
docs.integrationsDescription: Build webhooks that react to memories and real-time transcripts from Nooto.
docs.integrationsContent:
## Overview

Integration apps connect Nooto to external services using webhooks. When events happen in Nooto (a new memory is created, a real-time transcript segment is produced), Nooto sends an HTTP POST request to your server with the event data. Your server processes the data and optionally sends a response back.

All webhook requests include the header x-nooto-signature for payload verification, and your endpoint must respond within 30 seconds or the request will time out.

## Memory Creation Triggers

Your webhook is called whenever a memory is created after a conversation ends. This is the most common integration type.

### Webhook Payload Structure

Nooto sends a POST request to your endpoint with this JSON body:

- **memory_id** (string) — Unique identifier for the memory
- **created_at** (string) — ISO 8601 timestamp of when the memory was created
- **transcript** (string) — Full text transcript of the conversation
- **transcript_segments** (array) — Array of segment objects, each with: text, speaker ("SPEAKER_00", "SPEAKER_01"), start (float, seconds), end (float, seconds), is_user (boolean)
- **structured** (object) — AI-generated structured data with fields: title (string), overview (string), action_items (array of strings), category (string), emoji (string)
- **plugins_results** (array) — Results from other apps that ran on this memory
- **session_id** (string) — Unique session identifier for the conversation

### Example: Python FastAPI Memory Trigger

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
import httpx

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/nooto/memory")
async def handle_memory(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    memory_id = data["memory_id"]
    title = data["structured"]["title"]
    overview = data["structured"]["overview"]
    action_items = data["structured"]["action_items"]
    
    # Post to Slack
    slack_message = f"*'{'title'}'*\n'{'overview'}'"
    if action_items:
        slack_message += "\n\n*Action Items:*"
        for item in action_items:
            slack_message += f"\n- '{'item'}'"
    
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL, json='{'"text": slack_message'}')
    
    return '{'"status": "ok"'}'

### Response Format

Your endpoint should return a JSON response. If you want Nooto to display a message to the user, return:

- **message** (string) — Text to show the user as a notification

Return an empty 200 response if you do not want to notify the user.

## Real-time Transcript Processors

Your webhook receives live transcript segments as a conversation is happening. This enables real-time reactions.

### How It Works

1. The user starts a conversation with your app enabled
2. As audio is transcribed, Nooto sends transcript segments to your webhook every few seconds
3. Your server processes the segments and optionally returns a response
4. If you return a message, it appears as a notification to the user in real time

### Webhook Payload Structure

Nooto sends a POST request with this JSON body:

- **session_id** (string) — Unique session identifier, consistent for the entire conversation
- **segments** (array) — Array of new transcript segments since the last request, each with: text (string), speaker (string), start (float), end (float), is_user (boolean)

Important: segments are incremental. You may receive overlapping or corrected segments as the speech recognition refines its output. Use the start/end timestamps to deduplicate.

### Example: Python FastAPI Transcript Processor

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

session_context = '{''}'  # Track state per session

@app.post("/nooto/transcript")
async def handle_transcript(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    session_id = data["session_id"]
    segments = data["segments"]
    
    # Accumulate transcript for this session
    if session_id not in session_context:
        session_context[session_id] = []
    session_context[session_id].extend(segments)
    
    # Check for keywords
    full_text = " ".join(s["text"] for s in segments)
    if "action item" in full_text.lower():
        return '{'"message": "Action item detected! I will track this."'}'
    
    return '{''}'  # No notification

### Response Format

Return a JSON response with an optional message field:

- **message** (string, optional) — Text to display as a real-time notification to the user

Return an empty 200 response or an empty JSON object to acknowledge without notifying.

## Implementation Tips

- **Use session_id for state** — Real-time transcript requests for the same conversation share a session_id. Use this to accumulate context across multiple requests.
- **Deduplicate segments** — Speech recognition may refine previous segments. Use start/end timestamps as keys to avoid processing the same speech twice.
- **Fail gracefully** — If your server cannot process a request, return a 200 with an empty body. Returning errors may cause Nooto to retry or disable your app for the session.
- **Keep responses fast** — Your endpoint should respond within 5 seconds for real-time transcripts. For memory triggers, you have up to 30 seconds.
- **Idempotency** — Memory triggers may be retried. Use memory_id to ensure you do not process the same memory twice.

## Setting Up Your Server

Any HTTP server that accepts POST requests works. For development, use ngrok to expose your local server to the internet:

1. Run your server locally (e.g., uvicorn main:app --port 8000)
2. In a separate terminal, run: ngrok http 8000
3. Copy the ngrok HTTPS URL (e.g., https://abc123.ngrok.io)
4. Use this URL as your webhook endpoint when creating your app in Nooto

For production, deploy to any cloud provider (Railway, Render, Fly.io, AWS, GCP) and use your public HTTPS URL.

## Testing with Developer Mode

Enable Developer Mode in Nooto settings to test integrations:

1. Create your app with your webhook URL
2. Install it on your own account
3. Start a conversation to trigger real-time transcript webhooks
4. End the conversation to trigger memory creation webhooks
5. Check your server logs and the Developer Mode request log in the app
docs.chatToolsDescription: Add custom tools that Nooto's AI can call during conversations on behalf of users.
docs.chatToolsContent:
## Overview

Chat tools let you extend Nooto's AI with custom capabilities that it can invoke during conversations. When a user asks Nooto to do something that matches one of your tools, the AI sends a structured request to your endpoint and incorporates your response into its reply.

The flow works like this:

1. User says something like "Search Slack for the Q4 budget discussion"
2. Nooto's AI recognizes this matches your "search_slack" tool
3. The AI sends a POST request to your endpoint with structured parameters
4. Your server processes the request (searches Slack) and returns results
5. Nooto's AI incorporates the results into a natural language response to the user

## Tool Manifest

You define your tools by hosting a manifest file at /.well-known/omi-tools.json on your server. Nooto fetches this manifest to discover your tools.

### Manifest Structure

The manifest is a JSON array of tool objects. Each tool has:

- **name** (string, required) — Unique identifier for the tool, lowercase with underscores (e.g., "search_slack")
- **description** (string, required) — What the tool does. The AI uses this to decide when to call your tool, so be specific and descriptive.
- **parameters** (object, required) — A JSON Schema object defining the input parameters your tool expects. The AI will extract these from the user's message.
- **endpoint** (string, required) — The full URL that Nooto should POST to when calling this tool.
- **auth** (string, optional) — Authentication type: "none", "bearer", or "oauth". Defaults to "none".

### Example Manifest

Hosted at https://your-server.com/.well-known/omi-tools.json:

[
  '{'
    "name": "search_slack",
    "description": "Search for messages in Slack channels. Use this when the user wants to find a specific message or conversation in Slack.",
    "parameters": '{'
      "type": "object",
      "properties": '{'
        "query": '{'"type": "string", "description": "The search query"'}',
        "channel": '{'"type": "string", "description": "Optional channel name to search in"'}'
      '}',
      "required": ["query"]
    '}',
    "endpoint": "https://your-server.com/tools/search_slack"
  '}',
  '{'
    "name": "create_task",
    "description": "Create a new task in the user's task manager. Use this when the user wants to add a to-do item or task.",
    "parameters": '{'
      "type": "object",
      "properties": '{'
        "title": '{'"type": "string", "description": "The task title"'}',
        "due_date": '{'"type": "string", "description": "Due date in YYYY-MM-DD format"'}',
        "priority": '{'"type": "string", "enum": ["low", "medium", "high"]'}'
      '}',
      "required": ["title"]
    '}',
    "endpoint": "https://your-server.com/tools/create_task"
  '}'
]

## Handling Tool Calls

When Nooto calls your tool, it sends a POST request to your endpoint.

### Request Format

The request body contains:

- **tool_name** (string) — The name of the tool being called
- **parameters** (object) — The parameters extracted by the AI, matching your JSON Schema
- **user_id** (string) — The Nooto user ID making the request
- **session_id** (string) — The current conversation session ID

### Response Format

Return a JSON object with:

- **result** (string, required) — The result text. The AI will interpret this and present it naturally to the user.
- **error** (string, optional) — If something went wrong, return an error message instead of a result.

### Example: Python FastAPI Tool Server

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/tools/search_slack")
async def search_slack(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    query = data["parameters"]["query"]
    channel = data["parameters"].get("channel")
    
    # Your Slack search logic here
    results = perform_slack_search(query, channel)
    
    if not results:
        return '{'"result": "No messages found matching your query."'}'
    
    formatted = "\n".join(f"#'{'r['channel']'}' - '{'r['author']'}': '{'r['text']'}'" for r in results)
    return '{'"result": f"Found '{'len(results)'}' messages:\n'{'formatted'}'"'}'

@app.post("/tools/create_task")
async def create_task(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    title = data["parameters"]["title"]
    due_date = data["parameters"].get("due_date")
    priority = data["parameters"].get("priority", "medium")
    
    # Your task creation logic here
    task_id = create_task_in_manager(title, due_date, priority)
    
    return '{'"result": f"Task created: '{'title'}' (ID: '{'task_id'}', priority: '{'priority'}')"'}'

### Example: Node.js Express Tool Server

const express = require("express");
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

app.post("/tools/search_slack", async (req, res) => '{'
  const '{' query, channel '}' = req.body.parameters;
  const results = await performSlackSearch(query, channel);
  
  if (!results.length) '{'
    return res.json('{' result: "No messages found." '}');
  '}'
  
  const formatted = results.map(r => `#$'{'r.channel'}' - $'{'r.author'}': $'{'r.text'}'`).join("\n");
  res.json('{' result: `Found $'{'results.length'}' messages:\n$'{'formatted'}'` '}');
'}');

app.listen(8000);

## Proactive Chat Messages

In addition to responding to tool calls, you can proactively send messages to a user's Nooto chat using the API:

POST https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/'{'app_id'}'/chat/send

Headers: Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY, Content-Type: application/json

Body: '{'"user_id": "user_123", "message": "Reminder: your standup starts in 5 minutes!"'}'

This is useful for sending alerts, reminders, or status updates from your integration.

## Best Practices

- **Write descriptive tool descriptions** — The AI uses your description to decide when to call your tool. "Search for messages in Slack" is good. "Slack search" is too vague.
- **Use JSON Schema validation** — Define required fields, types, enums, and descriptions in your parameter schema. This helps the AI extract the right data.
- **Return human-readable results** — The AI will interpret your result text and present it to the user. Return clear, structured text, not raw JSON.
- **Keep response times under 10 seconds** — Users are waiting in a chat interface. If your tool is slow, return a "processing" message and use proactive chat to follow up.
- **Handle missing parameters gracefully** — The AI may not always extract every parameter. Check for None/null values and use sensible defaults.
- **Return helpful errors** — If a search returns nothing, say "No results found for X" instead of returning an empty result. The AI will communicate this better.

## Testing and Troubleshooting

1. Host your manifest at /.well-known/omi-tools.json and verify it is accessible
2. Create your app in Nooto and provide your server's base URL
3. Enable Developer Mode to see raw tool call requests and responses
4. Test each tool by asking Nooto natural language questions that should trigger it
5. Check the Developer Mode logs if the AI is not calling your tool (usually a description issue)
docs.audioStreamingDescription: Receive raw audio bytes from Nooto for custom speech processing and analysis.
docs.audioStreamingContent:
## Overview

Audio streaming lets your app receive raw audio data from the Nooto device or app in real time. This is the lowest-level integration, giving you direct access to the audio stream for custom speech-to-text engines, voice activity detection, speaker recognition, or any audio analysis.

## Technical Specifications

- **Format** — PCM16 (16-bit signed integers, little-endian)
- **Sample Rate** — 16000 Hz (16 kHz) by default. Some devices may send 8000 Hz.
- **Channels** — Mono (1 channel)
- **Chunk Size** — Variable, typically 320-4096 bytes per chunk (10-128ms of audio)
- **Protocol** — WebSocket connection
- **Encoding** — Raw bytes (binary WebSocket frames)

Each audio chunk contains raw PCM16 samples. At 16 kHz with 16-bit samples, one second of audio is 32,000 bytes.

## Setting Up Audio Streaming

1. Create your app in Nooto and select Audio Streaming as the capability
2. Provide your WebSocket endpoint URL (e.g., wss://your-server.com/audio)
3. When a user enables your app and starts a conversation, Nooto opens a WebSocket connection to your endpoint
4. Audio chunks are sent as binary frames
5. The connection closes when the conversation ends

## Processing Audio: Convert to WAV

To work with the raw PCM16 audio, you often need to convert it to WAV format for processing with libraries like Whisper, SpeechRecognition, or any audio analysis tool.

import io
import wave
import struct

def pcm16_to_wav(pcm_bytes, sample_rate=16000):
    """Convert raw PCM16 bytes to a WAV file in memory."""
    wav_buffer = io.BytesIO()
    with wave.open(wav_buffer, "wb") as wav_file:
        wav_file.setnchannels(1)  # Mono
        wav_file.setsampwidth(2)  # 16-bit = 2 bytes
        wav_file.setframerate(sample_rate)
        wav_file.writeframes(pcm_bytes)
    wav_buffer.seek(0)
    return wav_buffer

## Processing Audio: Accumulate Chunks

Audio arrives in small chunks. For most processing tasks, you need to accumulate chunks into larger buffers before processing.

import asyncio
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket

app = FastAPI()

@app.websocket("/audio")
async def audio_stream(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()
    audio_buffer = bytearray()
    CHUNK_DURATION_SECONDS = 5
    BYTES_PER_SECOND = 16000 * 2  # 16kHz * 16-bit
    BUFFER_SIZE = CHUNK_DURATION_SECONDS * BYTES_PER_SECOND
    
    try:
        while True:
            data = await websocket.receive_bytes()
            audio_buffer.extend(data)
            
            if len(audio_buffer) >= BUFFER_SIZE:
                # Process 5 seconds of audio
                wav = pcm16_to_wav(bytes(audio_buffer[:BUFFER_SIZE]))
                result = process_audio(wav)  # Your processing function
                audio_buffer = audio_buffer[BUFFER_SIZE:]
                
                # Optionally send results back
                if result:
                    await websocket.send_json('{'"transcript": result'}')
    except Exception:
        pass  # Connection closed

## Example: Custom ASR with Whisper

import whisper

model = whisper.load_model("base")

def process_audio(wav_buffer):
    """Transcribe audio using OpenAI Whisper."""
    import numpy as np
    import soundfile as sf
    
    audio_data, sample_rate = sf.read(wav_buffer)
    result = model.transcribe(audio_data)
    return result["text"]

## Example: Voice Activity Detection

Use audio streaming to detect when someone is speaking versus silence:

def detect_speech(pcm_bytes, threshold=500):
    """Simple energy-based voice activity detection."""
    samples = struct.unpack(f"<'{'len(pcm_bytes)//2'}'h", pcm_bytes)
    energy = sum(abs(s) for s in samples) / len(samples)
    return energy > threshold

For production VAD, consider libraries like webrtcvad or silero-vad which provide much more accurate results.

## Example: Speaker Embeddings

Use audio streaming to generate speaker embeddings for speaker identification:

def get_speaker_embedding(wav_buffer):
    """Generate speaker embedding using a pre-trained model."""
    from speechbrain.pretrained import EncoderClassifier
    classifier = EncoderClassifier.from_hparams(source="speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb")
    signal = classifier.load_audio(wav_buffer)
    embedding = classifier.encode_batch(signal)
    return embedding.squeeze().numpy()

## Best Practices

- **Buffer appropriately** — Process audio in chunks of 1-5 seconds. Processing every individual chunk (10-128ms) is too granular for most tasks.
- **Handle backpressure** — If your processing is slow, audio chunks will queue up. Consider dropping older chunks if you fall too far behind.
- **Clean up resources** — Free audio buffers and close file handles after processing. Audio data accumulates quickly in memory.
- **Use async I/O** — Audio streaming is inherently asynchronous. Use async WebSocket libraries (FastAPI, websockets) instead of synchronous ones.
- **Test with real audio** — Synthetic audio test data often does not capture background noise, echo, or variable speech patterns. Test with real Nooto recordings.
- **Monitor memory** — At 32 KB/second, a one-hour conversation produces about 115 MB of raw audio. Ensure your server can handle the memory requirements for your buffer strategy.
docs.notificationsDescription: Send push notifications and proactive messages to Nooto users from your app.
docs.notificationsContent:
## Overview

Nooto provides two ways for your app to send notifications to users:

- **Direct Notifications API** — Send push notifications to a specific user at any time
- **Proactive Notifications** — Trigger notifications from webhook responses during memory creation or transcript processing

## Direct Notifications API

Send a push notification to any user who has your app installed.

### Endpoint

POST https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/'{'app_id'}'/notifications/send

### Authentication

Include your app API key in the Authorization header:

Authorization: Bearer YOUR_APP_API_KEY

You receive your API key when you create your app in the Nooto developer portal.

### Request Body

- **user_id** (string, required) — The Nooto user ID to notify
- **title** (string, required) — Notification title (max 100 characters)
- **body** (string, required) — Notification body text (max 500 characters)
- **action_url** (string, optional) — Deep link URL to open when the notification is tapped

### Example: Python

import httpx

async def send_notification(user_id, title, body):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        response = await client.post(
            f"https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/'{'APP_ID'}'/notifications/send",
            headers='{'"Authorization": f"Bearer '{'API_KEY'}'"'}',
            json='{'
                "user_id": user_id,
                "title": title,
                "body": body
            '}'
        )
        return response.json()

### Example: Node.js

const axios = require("axios");

async function sendNotification(userId, title, body) '{'
  const response = await axios.post(
    `https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/$'{'APP_ID'}'/notifications/send`,
    '{' user_id: userId, title, body '}',
    '{' headers: '{' Authorization: `Bearer $'{'API_KEY'}'` '}' '}'
  );
  return response.data;
'}'

## Proactive Notifications from Webhooks

When your webhook processes a memory creation trigger or real-time transcript, you can include a notification in your response.

### From Memory Triggers

Return a message field in your webhook response:

'{'"message": "3 action items synced to your task manager!"'}'

This displays as an in-app notification to the user.

### From Real-time Transcripts

Return a message field in your transcript webhook response:

'{'"message": "Heads up: the client mentioned a competitor. Check the transcript."'}'

This appears as a real-time notification while the conversation is happening.

### Template Variables

You can use these variables in your notification messages, and Nooto will replace them:

- **'{'user_name'}'** — The user's display name
- **'{'memory_title'}'** — Title of the memory (memory triggers only)
- **'{'app_name'}'** — Your app's name

## Rate Limits

- **Direct API** — 100 notifications per user per hour, 10,000 total per app per hour
- **Webhook responses** — 1 notification per webhook response (the message field)
- **Burst limit** — Maximum 10 notifications per user in a 1-minute window

Exceeding rate limits returns a 429 status code. Implement exponential backoff in your retry logic.

## Complete Example: FastAPI Notification Server

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
import httpx

app = FastAPI()
APP_ID = "your-app-id"
API_KEY = "your-api-key"

@app.post("/nooto/memory")
async def handle_memory(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    action_items = data["structured"]["action_items"]
    
    if len(action_items) > 0:
        # Sync action items to your task manager
        synced = sync_to_task_manager(action_items)
        return '{'"message": f"'{'synced'}' action items synced to your task manager."'}'
    
    return '{''}'

@app.post("/send-reminder")
async def send_reminder(user_id: str, text: str):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            f"https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/'{'APP_ID'}'/notifications/send",
            headers='{'"Authorization": f"Bearer '{'API_KEY'}'"'}',
            json='{'"user_id": user_id, "title": "Reminder", "body": text'}'
        )
    return '{'"status": "sent"'}'

## Complete Example: Express Notification Server

const express = require("express");
const axios = require("axios");

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

const APP_ID = "your-app-id";
const API_KEY = "your-api-key";

app.post("/nooto/memory", async (req, res) => '{'
  const actionItems = req.body.structured.action_items;
  if (actionItems.length > 0) '{'
    const synced = await syncToTaskManager(actionItems);
    return res.json('{' message: `$'{'synced'}' action items synced.` '}');
  '}'
  res.json('{''}');
'}');

app.post("/send-reminder", async (req, res) => '{'
  const '{' userId, text '}' = req.body;
  await axios.post(
    `https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/$'{'APP_ID'}'/notifications/send`,
    '{' user_id: userId, title: "Reminder", body: text '}',
    '{' headers: '{' Authorization: `Bearer $'{'API_KEY'}'` '}' '}'
  );
  res.json('{' status: "sent" '}');
'}');

app.listen(8000);

## Error Codes

- **200** — Notification sent successfully
- **400** — Invalid request body (missing required fields)
- **401** — Invalid or missing API key
- **403** — User does not have your app installed
- **404** — User not found
- **429** — Rate limit exceeded
- **500** — Internal server error

## Troubleshooting

- **Notifications not arriving?** — Verify the user has your app installed and has push notifications enabled in their device settings.
- **Getting 403 errors?** — The user may have uninstalled your app. Check installation status before sending.
- **Rate limited?** — Batch non-urgent notifications. Send a daily digest instead of individual updates.
- **Notifications delayed?** — Push notification delivery depends on the device OS. iOS may batch notifications in low-power mode.
docs.oauthDescription: Authenticate Nooto users with your service using the OAuth 2.0 flow.
docs.oauthContent:
## Overview

If your app needs to access an external service on behalf of the user (e.g., their Slack workspace, Google Drive, or CRM), you can implement OAuth to securely connect their account. Nooto supports the standard OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow.

## How the OAuth Flow Works

The OAuth flow connects a Nooto user's account to your external service in four steps:

### Step 1: User Initiates Connection

When a user installs your app, Nooto redirects them to your authorization URL. The redirect includes:

- **client_id** — Your app's client ID
- **redirect_uri** — The Nooto callback URL: https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback
- **state** — A unique token that ties this request to the user's Nooto session. You must pass this back unchanged.
- **scope** — The permissions your app is requesting (defined by you)

Example URL that Nooto opens:
https://your-service.com/oauth/authorize?client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&redirect_uri=https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback&state=abc123&scope=read+write

### Step 2: User Authorizes

The user logs into your service and approves the permissions. Your service redirects back to the Nooto callback URL with an authorization code:

https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback?code=AUTH_CODE&state=abc123

### Step 3: Token Exchange

Nooto exchanges the authorization code for access and refresh tokens by calling your token endpoint:

POST https://your-service.com/oauth/token
Body: grant_type=authorization_code, code=AUTH_CODE, client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID, client_secret=YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET, redirect_uri=https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback

Your token endpoint returns:

- **access_token** (string) — Token for API calls
- **refresh_token** (string, optional) — Token to get new access tokens
- **expires_in** (integer) — Token lifetime in seconds
- **token_type** (string) — Usually "Bearer"

### Step 4: Setup Complete

Nooto stores the tokens securely and includes the access token in subsequent webhook requests to your app via the Authorization header.

## Implementing the OAuth Server

### Python FastAPI Example

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse
import secrets
import httpx

app = FastAPI()

# In-memory store (use a database in production)
auth_codes = '{''}'
token_store = '{''}'

NOOTO_CALLBACK = "https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback"

@app.get("/oauth/authorize")
async def authorize(client_id: str, redirect_uri: str, state: str, scope: str = "read"):
    # Show your login/consent page here. For this example, auto-approve:
    code = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    auth_codes[code] = '{'"client_id": client_id, "scope": scope, "state": state'}'
    return RedirectResponse(f"'{'redirect_uri'}'?code='{'code'}'&state='{'state'}'")

@app.post("/oauth/token")
async def token(request: Request):
    form = await request.form()
    code = form.get("code")
    
    if code not in auth_codes:
        return '{'"error": "invalid_grant"'}'
    
    access_token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    refresh_token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    token_store[access_token] = auth_codes.pop(code)
    
    return '{'
        "access_token": access_token,
        "refresh_token": refresh_token,
        "expires_in": 3600,
        "token_type": "Bearer"
    '}'

### Node.js Express Example

const express = require("express");
const crypto = require("crypto");

const app = express();
app.use(express.urlencoded('{' extended: true '}'));
app.use(express.json());

const authCodes = new Map();
const tokenStore = new Map();

const NOOTO_CALLBACK = "https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback";

app.get("/oauth/authorize", (req, res) => '{'
  const '{' client_id, redirect_uri, state, scope '}' = req.query;
  const code = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
  authCodes.set(code, '{' client_id, scope, state '}');
  res.redirect(`$'{'redirect_uri'}'?code=$'{'code'}'&state=$'{'state'}'`);
'}');

app.post("/oauth/token", (req, res) => '{'
  const '{' code '}' = req.body;
  if (!authCodes.has(code)) '{'
    return res.status(400).json('{' error: "invalid_grant" '}');
  '}'
  
  const accessToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
  const refreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
  tokenStore.set(accessToken, authCodes.get(code));
  authCodes.delete(code);
  
  res.json('{'
    access_token: accessToken,
    refresh_token: refreshToken,
    expires_in: 3600,
    token_type: "Bearer"
  '}');
'}');

app.listen(8000);

## Configuring OAuth in Your App

When creating your app in Nooto, provide these OAuth settings:

- **Authorization URL** — Where Nooto redirects users to authorize (e.g., https://your-service.com/oauth/authorize)
- **Token URL** — Where Nooto exchanges codes for tokens (e.g., https://your-service.com/oauth/token)
- **Client ID** — Your app's public identifier
- **Client Secret** — Your app's secret key (stored securely by Nooto)
- **Scopes** — Space-separated list of permissions your app needs

## Checking Setup Completion

After a user completes OAuth, your webhook requests will include the Authorization header with their access token. You can verify the connection is active by checking for this header:

def is_user_connected(request):
    auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")
    return auth_header is not None and auth_header.startswith("Bearer ")

## Security Best Practices

- **Always validate the state parameter** — Compare the state in the callback with the state you sent. This prevents CSRF attacks.
- **Use HTTPS everywhere** — All OAuth endpoints must be HTTPS. Nooto will not redirect to HTTP URLs.
- **Keep client secrets secure** — Never expose your client secret in frontend code or client-side applications.
- **Implement token refresh** — Access tokens should expire. Provide a refresh token so Nooto can get new access tokens without re-authorizing the user.
- **Limit scopes** — Only request the minimum permissions your app needs.
- **Handle token revocation** — Provide a way for users to disconnect their account. Revoke tokens when they uninstall your app.

## Troubleshooting

- **Redirect URI mismatch** — Ensure the redirect_uri in your authorization URL exactly matches https://api.nooto.app/v1/integrations/callback
- **State parameter lost** — You must return the state parameter unchanged in the callback redirect. If it is missing or modified, Nooto will reject the callback.
- **Token exchange failing** — Verify your token endpoint accepts POST with form-encoded body (application/x-www-form-urlencoded). Check that client_id and client_secret match.
- **Tokens expiring** — If your access tokens expire and you provide a refresh token, Nooto will automatically refresh them. If refresh fails, the user will be prompted to re-authorize.
docs.submittingDescription: Prepare, submit, and publish your app to the Nooto App Store.
docs.submittingContent:
## Before You Submit

Review this checklist before submitting your app:

- **Functionality** — Your app works reliably. For integration apps, your webhook endpoint is deployed, stable, and responds within the timeout limits (5 seconds for real-time, 30 seconds for memory triggers).
- **Error handling** — Your app handles errors gracefully and does not crash or return 500 errors under normal use.
- **Description** — You have a clear, accurate description of what your app does and how to use it.
- **Icon** — A square icon, 512x512 pixels minimum, in PNG or JPG format.
- **Category** — You have selected the correct category: Productivity, Health, Education, Entertainment, Business, Social, Utilities, or Developer Tools.
- **Privacy** — You have documented what data your app accesses, how it is stored, and whether it is shared with third parties.
- **Testing** — You have tested with multiple conversation types, different lengths, and edge cases (empty conversations, very long conversations, multiple speakers).
- **OAuth** — If your app uses OAuth, the flow completes successfully and handles errors (user denies, token expired).

## Submission Steps

1. Open the Nooto app on your phone
2. Go to your profile and find your app under "My Apps"
3. Tap your app and then tap "Submit for Review"
4. Fill in the submission form:

- **App Name** — Clear, descriptive name (max 30 characters)
- **Short Description** — One sentence explaining what it does (max 100 characters)
- **Full Description** — Detailed explanation with use cases and setup instructions
- **Category** — Select from the available categories
- **Icon** — Upload your 512x512 icon
- **Screenshots** — Up to 5 screenshots showing your app in action (optional but recommended)
- **Privacy URL** — Link to your privacy policy (required for integration apps)
- **Website URL** — Link to your app's website or documentation (optional)

5. Review and submit

## Community Guidelines

Your app must comply with these guidelines:

- **No harmful content** — Apps must not generate, promote, or facilitate harmful, illegal, or discriminatory content
- **User consent** — Apps must clearly inform users what data is collected and how it is used
- **No spam** — Apps must not send excessive or irrelevant notifications
- **Accuracy** — Your app description must accurately reflect what the app does
- **Data minimization** — Only collect and process the data your app needs to function
- **No impersonation** — Do not impersonate other apps, services, or individuals
- **Reliability** — Integration apps must maintain reasonable uptime. Repeated downtime will result in delisting.

## Review Process

The Nooto team reviews every submission. Here is what to expect:

- **Timeline** — Reviews typically take 2-5 business days
- **Testing** — The review team will install and test your app with real conversations
- **Feedback** — If changes are needed, you will receive specific feedback on what to fix
- **Resubmission** — After making requested changes, resubmit for another review
- **Approval** — Once approved, your app is published to the store within 24 hours

## Common Rejection Reasons

- **Broken functionality** — The app crashes, times out, or does not work as described
- **Vague description** — The description does not clearly explain what the app does
- **Missing privacy policy** — Integration apps must have a privacy policy URL
- **Excessive permissions** — The app requests more data or capabilities than it needs
- **Poor error handling** — The app returns errors or confusing messages during normal use
- **Inappropriate content** — The app generates or facilitates harmful content
- **Duplicate app** — An identical or nearly identical app already exists

## After Publishing

Once your app is live on the Nooto App Store:

- **Monitor performance** — Check your webhook logs for errors and slow responses
- **Read reviews** — Users can rate and review your app. Respond to feedback and improve.
- **Push updates** — You can update your app's prompt, webhook URL, or description at any time without re-review. Major changes (new capabilities, OAuth) require a new review.
- **Analytics** — View install counts, active users, and usage metrics in your developer dashboard
- **Promote your app** — Share your app link (https://apps.nooto.app/app/YOUR_APP_ID) on social media, in blog posts, and with your community
- **Join the community** — Connect with other developers at https://discord.nooto.app

download.heroDescription: Download Nooto for your phone, desktop, or use it in any browser. Your conversations sync seamlessly across all devices.
download.oneAccountDescription: Start a conversation on your pendant, review the summary on your phone, search it later on your Mac. Everything stays in sync.
download.feat6: Works with or without the pendant
download.readyDescription: Download Nooto and start capturing your conversations today.
